name: tias
content: I’m feeling sad—irrationally sad—about everyone graduating. I won’t quite throw up yet but we are getting close. This feels like grief, so much like grief. I cannot fathom the thought of a figurine from my battlefield life ceasing. She will be gone.

It would be overly dramatic to say that I won’t ever see her again. I probably will bump into her a few years from now, at some stupid reunion, by which time the memories will have faded. Reunion implies present disunion preceded by past union. It is this slippage between past and present that is bringing me to tears. My internal response to this grief is to label it irrational (see first line). I am some ten thousand kilometers away and she is off getting high with her other friends. I saw her often, often enough that her fuck-ass living room—table and always-dirty glasses and all—is etched into the past I see as light, and that I see as dark. I remember throwing away her handwritten DNR before EMS showed up the day she attempted suicide. At the time I thought about what might have happened if I were later than I was. I remember talking to her father over the phone, over a video call, as they angled and hauled her stretcher down spiraling stairs. He was crying. He thought he’d never see her again. Now he’s dead, and she won’t ever see him again. She’s going to move—in two days or so—back to where she came from: Dearborn, Michigan. This, of course, isn’t really where she’s from, but that is excusably okay for now.

Some five thousand of her batchmates are graduating, too. Some of these started as I did and leapfrogged ahead without ever saying goodbye. Most of them I do not remember let alone miss. I still grieve them. From behind the screen I have seen hundreds upon hundreds of graduating smiles. I have seen not one pair of eyes breaking down, and this is by design; we hide our flaws. I too am suppressing a rock in my throat at the moment. I am not as good at veiling as are they, and especially as is she. I spoke to her over the phone a few minutes ago. Between the filler words and usual dull expressions of mute there was silence. I never knew her to be a silent one. I do not know why I write of her as if she’s dead, or dying. She will start a new life. I look at the one she’s graduating from, so full of irresponsible nights, so drunk, high. She was the opposite of put together. Not one thing in her kitchen was where it would’ve belonged. Her fridge had someone else’s half-finished fancy liquor, the cabinet something cheaper which we’d turn upside-down and waterfall. Her dining table was always a mess. Her bedsheets were never very well folded. Stray charging cables snaked across her bed often.

I remember her makeup table, with a mirror she’d use to judge herself. I remember being upset at her for yassifying me, badly. I remember threatening to clog her toilet when she first told me she was getting an apartment. I remember the first time I saw her in person. It was a party freshman fall—she was at lots of parties those first two years. Towards the end she was paranoid of the image she’d made for herself, which truthfully I am in no unbiased position to observe. She was always worried what others thought of her; I presume she had a fairly glamorous day-life. She talked often of professors applauding her writing, of her politically incorrect club with its drunk debates, of her millions of pathways ahead. I wonder what she will do with all of that applause.

She is probably panicking at least a little bit about packing her shit up and moving it halfway across the breadth of the country, as she should be. Another friend of mine—also a girl—is moving down to the City to start a six-figure job. Another, from Vietnam, is graduating too but I have no idea where he’s going. It is partially this bit that stings me so fucking bad: I haven’t a clue of the lives of these figurines beyond surface and sometimes symbol. Thence for now we grieve only the one friend who gets to leave—who was there, metaphysically, in the first place such that she would have a standing to leave. We come back to the apartment. Across from the dining table were two long couches and a TV we never used. I have laid across both those couches, sofas, futons, whatever they’re called. I have laid on the floor flanked by the couches and the TV, next to an extension-cord-power-strip-thing.

Then there is the journey. The walk down from North Campus, the walk up to the Indian store with cheap wine, the walk back down. These scenes I will not have an excuse to see again. Not in that same way. Of course I will see again the entrance to the underground bar whose Bengali owner she was very good friends with. I will not see her outside that entrance again. I will not again be able to meekly nibble on the terribly inauthentic food she’d concoct. She will never again call me on a Tuesday night and ask if I wanted to drink. It is right now that I wish—like hell, like the scorching Sun—that I’d taken her up on that offer more times than I did. Her living room was dingy and dimly lit for the most part, and drinking there could be a depressing experience, especially given both of our predispositions thereto. I wish I’d been depressed with her more often.

I know for a fact that her depression persists. She does not let it show nor affect too much materially, but it persists and I will not be there to share it with her and she to share mine. My loads feel, or will feel, heavier now that I know that an escape is gone. I will miss bitching with her about the Cornell Daily Sun. I will miss her commentaries and cynicisms of the world. I will miss how she always pretended like everything was (going to be) perfect even if the present was a piece of shit.

I will also miss her mum. I’ve met her in person on only one occasion—that after her attempt—and have spoken to her much since. Her whole family had shown up. They were all so aggrieved, and that too passed. Then there was a time not too long ago, that we had to rescue her again. Her mum called me late at night, my friends and I were out, and we made a beeline to her place. She wouldn’t pick up calls or open doors. We did not know if she was alive. I did not know if she was alive. We broke in through the window. Thankfully she was only asleep. I too have alarmed my parents with such behavior in the past—I have been dealt with by police officers at my doorstep more than once. I’m glad that we didn’t do that with her. I’m glad that she was okay, and I was very upset with her for not indicating so in a more responsible manner. That too passed. She stopped locking her door—which is its own separate issue—and so we’d waltz in often, sometimes unannounced. It was two of my friends and I, over the last year, and her. The attempt was before this. My friends too miss her, I’m sure. They made their own memories with her too. The night we broke in, after I left, they ended up at a lighthouse where they stole a golfcart. I’m blessed beyond heaven to be a year above these two friends; I cannot fathom losing them before they lose me.

Her mother invited me once or twice to their place, in Dearborn, Michigan. I doubt she was being much more than courteous, but now we will be soon old enough to take trips to see each other. Maybe she will eventually move to D.C. as she says she will, or to the City, and maybe I’ll be close enough to see her sometimes, or once a year, or less. Toss-ups like these don’t amuse me. I might end up in Timbuktu, or worse yet, in India. Realistically, neither of us will be rich enough to jet around the other’s neighborhood anytime soon. Realistically, the chasm in my heart will feel less empty, and then lesser still. Nobody will take her place, ever, even if she truly does die, and I’m sure nobody will take mine, but time and space indeed will numb the pain. I say numb because she already is numb to much emotion. She is mature enough to not cry over this. I am not. In a grander scheme of things she will get a job and so will I, and even if we don’t forget we will cease to recall so often. This is the distance they all speak of.

And so, the tear-jerks will become softer and drier. I will accept the fact that she is if not dead then frozen away in an insurmountable block of ice. And of course, anything could happen. She could be deported back to where she truly came from. Her mother could found a billion-dollar Halal makeup brand. Clouds could burst and the earth quake. I could die. She could die. Anything could happen. I don’t know if to find solace in this. I don’t know if to be reassured. This is a heartbreak. The very passage of time has condemned me to heartbreak. I wish we could turn back time. I wish I could go back to that first party and give her the hug she so badly and obviously needed. I wish I didn’t love her so and I wish I had the means to bring my love for her to fruition at the command of a finger-snap. I wish I owned United Airlines.
I would not have called—in any of these years—Asfi my best friend. Today, now, I have moist eyes. Today, now, she is fucking off. Goodbye, asshole. I fucking love you.
